item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image is from here and is copyrighted.

The second image is from here, without being released under a CC compliant license. It is assumed not compliant.

As such, both images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The top of it looks like a face. Was that on purpose or is it just my paredolia talking? (Probably spelled that wrong)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you have the sobby wobbys?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
now i have the sads….

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this article, but I was confused by how the archived version refers to the filament material- or more accurately, how it doesn't refer to it. At all.

I got the impression that the printer originally didn't need filament- hence Dr. Grant's confused reaction to "filament jam", and no one being required to provide filament in the containment procedures, even when it was being used regularly- but that was never spelled out anywhere. If my interpretation is correct, then the archived article has completely left out one of the object's anomalous properties, which is very bad. If my interpretation is incorrect, then I don't understand how people were making models with this thing without providing filament to make them from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted. Although if the kid said cyka blyat at the last part I will be more than happy to give another upvote… If I can.
Interesting story nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, quickly changed that over to filament.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Filament. Take it from a guy who owns a 3D printer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, a google search returns with "filament" as the proper name…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe it is actually called toner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only question is…Is a 3D printer's material actually called 'toner'?

If it is, no problem.

If it isn't, you might want to find the right term.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What Marr965 said, first of all. Second of all, the way this thing was presented, I liked the first version we see, but then when I saw the second version and saw it saying "this thing is actually really nice and not a threat", my first thought was of the man-eating holly bush (at least I think it's holly, it's been a while), except not nearly as justifiable. Then I got to the part where one researcher guy was just introducing the new researcher guy to this object that requires SCP by turning it on and having a friendly conversation with it, and then something went wrong, and they responded, which resulted in the new SCP. I feel like no member of the Foundation would willingly put themselves in a position where that might happen - didn't they learn "just because something seems friendly doesn't mean we should treat it as such" from the teddy bear that makes replicas of itself? It just didn't feel like something that would really happen in the Foundation. The whole exchange was just too much like two normal guys talking about a new car or something - "cold not cruel" still means "cold", which means "indifferent". Also, "You killed my friend!"? Since when do Foundation staff members refer to each other as "friends"? And while we're on the subject, why did Alexi not register the fact that the researcher got upset because someone got killed? I get that it's a child, but if it can be taught different languages, it's not stupid.

My typical mentality is to upvote unless there's a serious reason not to, but even a novote would be extremely generous here, and I'm not feeling especially generous. It's just too incongruous with the Foundation across the board. Yes, it qualifies for SCP per se, but that's about it, and in this case, it's not enough even for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! I've tried to adjust the issue with the "infinite objects" confusion, and I've tried to make it a little less…. network heavy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Due to SCP-1146's ability to network, it is not to be allowed contact with any form of external network, and may only be allowed access to limited on-site networks for the purposes of testing.

network network network network. I know academic literature is often repetition-heavy, but that's a teensy bit too much, especially so early in the article.

In the event that an object is larger than SCP-1146, it will consume as much of the sample as it can

The grammar here is a bit wonky—makes it sound like the phenomenon happens if any object larger than 1146 exists at all.

Apart from that, I like this article a lot, especially how petulant the last e-mail sounds. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has anyone tried to explain to Alexei that killing people is wrong? You know, sat down, said that they didn't know what to do when the machine grabbed the guy's hand and unplugged it hoping that would stop it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, most people don't take well to being told they can't do stuff that seems fun or makes them special whenever they want/at all, so I suppose it'll happen pretty often.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why is it that we manage to piss off every sentient technological being the foundation comes into contact with? Of course, it is their own fault, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this article. As it began, I thought it was just going to be one of those old "kills people lol" artifacts with a tacked on human persona to get upvotes. I was pleasantly proven wrong. The way the story developed through the article was something I feel is very rare on this site. I really think that the addendum really were what made the article; without them, there would be no story, and this would've probably crashed an burned.

Unlike a lot of people on this thread, I actually really like the inclusion of the original containment procedures as part of the article. It really slams down the mantra, "We are cold, not cruel." They did what was required of them. They failed to see the possible dangers of the object because it had a humanoid conscience and paid dearly. They made a mistake, forgetting that the printer wasn't just sentient, but a child, liable to make childish mistakes and lash out at punishment. As such, they tried to fix the situation by dehumanizing the report, making sure that no one else makes that error again.

I read that articles are supposed to bring out an emotional reaction in the reader, and this one definitely did that. Heartily upvoted.

Now, that being said, there are still some things that don't seem quite right. Why is "Toner" always capitalized? Also,

"Come to life"

Doesn't seem to be clinical. Maybe if you replaced it with "animate" or something, it would work better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy… that think got into a network once.
Who knows WHAT did it see in that network that could help it escape later?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have also now changed the transcript, to reflect the Printer's character a bit better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've made some changes to the Article, in an attempt to make it "Pop" a little more. Any suggestions would be appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was exactly what I was aiming for on Alexi's behavior. I'm glad it worked for you. As for the format, I'm considering reworking a bit of it, as a number of people have said that they don't quite like the way it was presented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really dislike the way you presented the two different versions. In-univers, the original write-up before it went hostile might well be included for archival purposes, but it would more likely be just some notes on the original behavior. Even if the full presentation was preserved, I see no reason for it to require higher security clearance. If anything, it should be less secured than the current version because it is no longer relevant to containment.

That's the negative, on the positive I think you really nailed the characterization of Alexi. He behaves like a 9 year old who could make living toys would. The fact that chewing up living people to fuel his models doesn't seem wrong to him is the creepiest part of this. From his point of view, he was just playing and everyone was having a good time, then the staff started being mean to him for no reason. Now he wants to leave and they won't let him. So he makes dinosaur friends to help him. That could be my nephew's personality in that printer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: None taken, certainly not from you at least. You've got a decent concept here, and though I don't agree with the execution, it's doing very well, especially for a first-timer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please don't take my reply as an insult, it was never meant as such. I will take all of these into consideration for editing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am typically on my phone when making comments like that, and am thereby unable to elaborate effectively. It's coming up on midnight so I've only covered the portion before the first collapsible, but this should at least make my point:

+ show block
– hide block

Modifications include […] apparent lack of a requirement for a power source

Awkward phrasing

and a number of wires jutting

Questionable word choice ("jutting")

As the device lacks any form of opening in its case, X-ray imaging has been used to reveal a lack of internal components.

Awkward. Consider "Though no means of opening the object have been found, X-ray imaging…

[…] (Although it is currently unknown how it ascertains this.)

The parenthetical is a seperate thought and should probably be its own sentence, if retained at all.

[…] no biological material over one kilogram is placed […]

Phrasing; at minimum the ammount should be qualified as "over one kilogram in mass"

attack any and all humans

IMO unecessarily florid and a little redundant.

although requiring several shots to kill

Awkward

will create increasingly dangerous models as it moves

Word choice re: "dangerous". Perhaps "increasingly aggressive"?

SCP-1146 will continue this until it runs out of toner

Unqualified demonstrative adjective; perhaps "continue this behavior" or "continue this pattern"

unless there is a human around

Informal usage of "around"

I do apologize for not continuing, but I've not had much time to spare for the wiki of late. I hope that this at least demonstrates the nature of my complaint and/or "backs up my gripe".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It may actively want to escape, but as long as nobody is stupid enough to jam their hand into it, we should be fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although it is currently unknown how it knows this.

Nyerggh. I don't like this sentence.

I love this idea; very good pacing. However, shouldn't the Class be changed to Euclid? It's a sentient being that actively wants to escape. Still, very very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image of SCP-1146-1 is too obviously a figurine, because of it being attached to a base. Maybe the image caption should say that it was attached to a base after it vitrified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok. I just want to make this article as good as I can. It's my first SCP, so I'd like to leave a good impression with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tone is a hard thing to specifically pinpoint while it's very easy to feel, sometimes I can read an article and I know the tone is wrong but I'm not sure specifically why, this is a case that I think the tone might be a little off but I have no specifics examples of where it's off.

I still upvoted because I like the concept (even if it's similar to 914 but hateful and it wants to escape.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some people cite tone without giving any examples… Seems to be a common gripe that is hardly ever backed up…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about the tone was an issue for you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some noticeable issues with tone, and somethin, about the format in which this was presented bothers me. Downvote, albeit not by much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, although I had to read it twice to get it to click.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For those who are curious, this SCP was inspired by Megaman. I liked the concept of a helper robot, but found myself translating the idea to the real world, and adding some more interesting hooks to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly! If you were a 9 year old living at a boarding school, and suddenly everybody there seems to want you dead, you're going to try and fight your way out at any cost. It just wouldn't work the same way if it was just some personality-less AI.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The testing log is from a "Previous" version of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending doesn't really grab me. "Oh no, it ate a guy and now it hates us." Also, how the heck can you have a testing log for something whose actual functions are level-3 classified?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, the child personality offers the explanation for why it's acting the way it is - from its POV, it was just trying to gain a necessary supply, when suddenly its new friends tried to kill it! Now it wants to get away, and maybe find some new friends who won't be so mean…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting concept. I would say some sentences need polishing. Drop the Russian child - a printer acting on its own is creepy enough.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot about this that doesn't sit right with me.

The collapsible previous SCP is unnecessary and largely redundant. It could be a single sentence in the revised SCP.

The Russian child personality also seems gratuitous. I think the subject would be creepy on its own without needing a creepy child angle.

As stated before, this is Safe, not Euclid. Lock it away without toner and nothing bad will happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that second pic. Maybe move the first photo up a little?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoting. This is interesting enough, and I like the change in the SCP's behavior during its confinement. One thing nags at me though; how does it know how to make the series of dangerous models like scorpions and velociraptors? It looks like it has to have seen something or had it described to it to make it.

Did the SCP's creator sit down one day with a catalogue of vicious animals and describe them to it? It's kind of an interesting thought and would explain how messed up the person who would create this sort of thing is, but I'm not sure whether any sort of exposition like that would help or hinder the entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have only one issue with this, It seems that it was reclassified from Safe to Euclid because of the danger it now presents? Due to the fact that it is harmless when its toner is empty and does not do anything when no-one is within 5 meters, shouldn't it still be safe?

One other thing, Why does it have to be in an unlit cell?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think proper would be
Osvobodite etogo bednogo celoveka. (I'm not too good with latinzations)

EDIT: Ah good, you fixed it by removing that stuff. Even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok then. I can fix that. As for the latinization, I believe both Alexei and Alexiy are appropriate, as I have seen both used. Also, I do speak a bit of Russian, apparently it's just worse than I thought. I used a translator just to get a few words I didn't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Besplatno, chto bednyĭ chelovek!

Proper translation: Free [of charge], what poor person!
I LOL'd.
Also, russian words "bend" like latin, they don't stay the same as english. Pretty much all of the excerpts are speaking in an "Ugg hunt meat, ugg no hungry now." way.

Seriously, if you can't actually speak any russian, just include the translation. You aren't winning any points by showing us you can speak Google Translatorish.
Also, wouldn't the latinization be Alexei?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There we go. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I'll have to see why that is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I added a ratings module for you. Also, your second image appears broken.
